_meta: Ground-truth FAQ for the in-app assistant. The bot answers tool_help questions ONLY from this file. Keep answers factual and short. Do not add features that aren't in the tool.

faqs.a: It turns a survey into a consultant-ready Excel workbook. It reads your data, computes how people answered every question (single cuts), lets you slice questions against each other (cross cuts), and splits respondents into Winners vs Laggards on an outcome to see what differentiates them.

Three options: (1) raw data (.csv/.xlsx) plus a data map (.xlsx); (2) one combined Excel with data and map on separate sheets; (3) raw data plus a Word questionnaire (.docx) the tool reads to build the data map.
The breakdown of one question: each answer option, how many people chose it, and the percentage. For numeric questions it's the mean and spread; for NPS it's the NPS score.
A relationship between two questions — e.g. a cross-tab of two categorical questions, or the mean of a numeric question by a categorical segment.

A slicer at the top of each question's sheet in the Excel output. A reader can use it to restrict that sheet to a subgroup, e.g. only APAC respondents, without re-running anything. You pick which questions become Local Filters in step 2.

faqs.q: What's the difference between a Local Filter and the global filter?
faqs.a: A Local Filter slices one sheet in the output. The global filter restricts the entire analysis — every screen and sheet — to a subgroup.

It splits respondents into Winners and Laggards on an outcome you choose (e.g. revenue growth), then finds the questions where the two groups answer most differently, ranked by lift and gap. Cohorts and rates are computed in Python; AI only phrases the finding.

faqs.q: How do I define Winners and Laggards?
faqs.a: Four ways: by categorical option(s) chosen, by a numeric threshold, by quartile (top 25% vs bottom 25%), or by uploading a manual list of respondent IDs.
How many times more likely Winners are to choose an option than Laggards. A lift of 2x means Winners pick it twice as often.

faqs.q: Why does a cell get highlighted red or amber?
faqs.a: Red flags an unusually high value, amber an unusually low one, using a guarded statistical test. Flat or very small distributions are deliberately left unflagged to avoid false alarms.
A toggle on the single-cut screen. On: the single cut and its cross-cut builder stack in one column. Off: they sit side by side.
Single-cut workbook (everything), cross-cut-only workbook, filtered workbook (per-question filters you ticked), and the outcome segmented workbook (Winners vs Laggards side by side).

faqs.q: Which program should I open the workbook in?
faqs.a: Microsoft Excel desktop. Some live formulas don't resolve correctly in LibreOffice, Google Sheets, or Excel web.

faqs.q: Does the AI make up numbers?
faqs.a: No. Every number is computed in Python. AI only groups questions, suggests labels, and phrases takeaways, and any AI sentence with a number is re-checked against the computed table before you see it.

faqs.q: Why did the assistant refuse to confirm my hypothesis?
faqs.a: If the cross-cut it built has a very small base (fewer than 30 in a cell), it shows the table and gives only a tentative, hedged reading rather than a confident claim. It also never claims causation — only association.

faqs.q: How does the hypothesis tester work?
faqs.a: You state a hypothesis. The assistant maps it to two survey questions, runs the real cross-cut engine on your data, shows you the resulting table, and describes only what that table shows.
The number of respondents who gave a usable answer to that question — the base the percentages are calculated on.

faqs.q: Can I group questions into themed sheets?
faqs.a: Yes. In step 2 you choose whether questions are grouped into category sheets or kept on a single sheet. You can rename categories and move questions between them.
